planting.note: Plant bulbs 3-4 inches deep and 4-6 inches apart in sharply well-drained soil in fall, 4-8 weeks before first frost; drumstick allium is excellent planted in drifts through ornamental grasses, as the grass foliage conceals the dying allium leaves while the egg-shaped flower heads rise above for a naturalistic effect. Excellent drainage is essential — bulbs rot in heavy or waterlogged soils over winter.

directions: Plant bulbs 3-4 inches deep in sharply well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun in fall for June-July bloom; drumstick allium is one of the most distinctive of all ornamental alliums, producing tight, egg-shaped, 1-2 inch flower heads on wiry, 18-24-inch stems — the heads emerge green and gradually transform to deep burgundy-violet as the flowers open from base to tip, creating a two-toned effect that is uniquely ornamental and highly valued by floral designers. It blooms later than most alliums, bridging the gap between spring bulbs and summer perennials, and its slender profile makes it an outstanding candidate for weaving through clumps of ornamental grasses such as Stipa or Pennisetum, whose foliage disguises the dying allium leaves. The bulbs naturalize freely in well-drained or rocky soils and multiply over time to form good-sized colonies without becoming invasive. The spent flower heads dry to a warm brown on the stem and provide continued ornamental value into late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 4-5 inches deep and 8-10 inches apart in sharply well-drained, dry to average soil in fall, 4-8 weeks before first frost; Star of Persia demands excellent drainage and tolerates poor, dry, stony soils well — bulbs will rot in heavy clay or persistently moist conditions. Plant in large groups of 5 or more for the most spectacular display, and allow the giant dried seed heads to remain on the plant through summer for continued ornamental impact.

directions: Plant bulbs 4-5 inches deep in sharply well-drained, dry to average soil in full sun in fall for May-June bloom; Star of Persia produces the most spectacular flower head of any ornamental allium — enormous, globose umbels 8-12 inches in diameter composed of up to 100 individual metallic, star-shaped flowers in shimmering lilac-purple, each petal with a deeper central stripe, held on stiff, 12-18-inch stems above broad, strap-like, somewhat untidy basal leaves. The giant spherical heads are extraordinarily long-lasting, both on the plant and when cut and dried, and the papery star-shaped seed heads persist through summer and into autumn, turning a warm silvery-brown that is as beautiful as the flowers themselves. It performs best in hot, dry, well-drained sites that approximate its native Central Asian steppe habitat, and is outstanding in gravel gardens, rock gardens, and dry Mediterranean-style borders. It may be left to self-seed, and the resulting seedlings, while slow to reach flowering size, gradually build into naturalistic colonies.
sources: Chicago Botanic Garden Plant Evaluation Notes

planting.note: Plant bulbs 4-5 inches deep and 8-10 inches apart in well-drained, fertile to average soil in fall, 4-8 weeks before first frost; Purple Sensation allium is one of the most reliable and free-flowering of the large ornamental alliums and adapts to a range of well-drained garden soils. Plant toward the middle or back of the border — the large basal leaves die back before or as the flowers open, and surrounding foliage of perennials such as hostas, geraniums, or ornamental grasses will conceal the untidy leaves.

directions: Plant bulbs 4-5 inches deep in well-drained, fertile to average soil in full sun in fall for May bloom; Purple Sensation allium is among the most popular and widely grown of all ornamental alliums, producing dense, perfectly round, 3-4-inch globes of deep violet-purple flowers on tall, 24-36-inch stems in mid-spring — a vivid and eye-catching display that bridges late tulips and early summer perennials. The cultivar 'Purple Sensation' is particularly prized for its intense, saturated violet-purple color that does not fade as the flowers age, distinguishing it from the more commonly offered seed-grown Allium aflatunense strains which may show paler, more variable coloring. The broad, strap-like basal leaves emerge in early spring and die back as the flowers open, so siting near bushy perennials or ornamental grasses to disguise the foliage is recommended. The dried round seed heads are attractive and long-lasting on the stem, continuing to provide structural interest through summer.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs 2-3 inches deep and 3-5 inches apart in sharply well-drained, dry to average, preferably lean soil in fall, 4-8 weeks before first frost; Yellow Allium is native to dry, rocky Mediterranean and Central European hillsides and thrives in the hot, dry conditions of a rock garden, gravel planting, or the front of a dry sunny border. It self-seeds readily in suitable well-drained conditions and will naturalize freely to form expanding colonies over several years.

directions: Plant small bulbs 2-3 inches deep in sharply well-drained, dry, lean to average soil in full sun in fall for summer bloom; Yellow Allium is one of the very few ornamental alliums to offer yellow flowers, producing loose, airy, umbel-like heads of small, pendant, golden-yellow, bell-shaped flowers on slender 6-12-inch stems with attractive thread-like, blue-green foliage in midsummer, after the main flush of spring alliums has passed. The flowers are held on unequal pedicels that give the open umbel a spraying, graceful appearance quite different from the tight, geometric globes of the larger ornamental alliums, and they are attractive to bees and other small pollinators. It is best suited to rock gardens, sunny gravel plantings, dry stone walls, or the front of a hot, dry border where its small stature and love of lean, sharply drained soil can be accommodated; rich or moist soils encourage weak, lush growth and reduced flowering. It self-seeds freely in well-drained conditions and over time can naturalize into loose, informal drifts.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs 2-3 inches deep and 3-4 inches apart in moist but well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in fall, 4-8 weeks before first frost; Spanish hyacinth is exceptional among small spring bulbs for its genuine shade tolerance and thrives under the canopy of deciduous trees and shrubs where it receives bright indirect light but little direct sun. It naturalizes freely under trees where most spring bulbs struggle and is most effective planted in generous informal drifts of 20 or more.

directions: Plant small bulbs 2-3 inches deep in moist but well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in fall for May bloom; Spanish hyacinth is a charming and genuinely shade-tolerant small bulb from the Pyrenean mountains, closely related to Hyacinthus but far more tolerant of woodland conditions, producing slender 6-8-inch stems bearing loose, one-sided racemes of 6-12 pendant, bright blue, tubular bells with slightly flared mouths that closely resemble a miniature grape hyacinth in form but are a more vivid, pure blue. It is one of the most valuable of all shade-tolerant spring bulbs precisely because so few small bulbs bloom well in the deep, root-filled shade under established deciduous trees — a position where it thrives and naturalizes freely by offsets and occasional self-seeding into charming informal colonies. A white-flowered form ('Alba') is also available. Excellent for underplanting shrubs, naturalized in woodland gardens, or interplanted with ferns, hostas, and other shade perennials for a layered spring display.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3-4 inches deep and 4-6 inches apart in well-drained to moderately moist, average to humus-rich soil in fall, 4-8 weeks before first frost; Drooping Star-of-Bethlehem is excellent for naturalizing under deciduous trees and in dappled shade situations, where it spreads steadily and blooms reliably each spring with little or no maintenance. It is vigorous and can spread extensively by offsets in favorable conditions — site with care if spread needs to be controlled, but it is excellent for filling areas of dry or difficult shade.

directions: Plant bulbs 3-4 inches deep in well-drained to moderately moisture-retentive, average soil in partial to full shade under deciduous trees in fall for April-May bloom; Drooping Star-of-Bethlehem is the most shade-tolerant of the commonly grown ornithogalums and distinguished from the common O. umbellatum by its larger stature, later bloom time, and most distinctively by its elegantly arching, one-sided flower spikes bearing up to 20 large, nodding, white flowers with broad silvery-white inner petals and conspicuous green outer stripes that give the backs of the flowers a bold two-toned appearance — hence the species name nutans, meaning nodding. It blooms in April and May under deciduous trees and naturalizes freely by bulb offsets in suitable shaded conditions, forming expanding colonies year after year in dry or semi-dry shade situations where many other plants fail. The flowers are excellent for cutting and last well in a vase. All parts of this plant are toxic if ingested; handle with normal precautions and site away from areas accessible to children and pets.
